question: [File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / IOracle.price] Can an unprivileged market creator deploy a market whose CollateralParams.oracle returns a price not normalized to ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE=1e36 for the collateral token's actual decimals (e.g., 6-decimal USDC collateral with oracle returning price as if 18-decimal), causing isHealthy to compute maxDebt += collateral[i].mulDivDown(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivDown(lltv, WAD) with a 1e12 inflation factor, making every borrower appear 1e12x healthier than reality, so undercollateralized borrowers are never liquidatable? Preconditions: market created with 6-decimal collateral token and oracle that returns price in 18-decimal units without adjusting for the 1e12 decimal gap; borrower supplies minimal collateral and borrows near LLTV. Call sequence: touchMarket -> supplyCollateral -> take (borrow) -> isHealthy returns true despite debt >> maxDebt. Invariant tested: unhealthy positions must be liquidatable; IERC20 interface has no decimals() so protocol cannot self-correct. Scoped impact: invalid borrowers stay healthy, bad debt accumulates undetected. Proof idea: deploy MockOracle returning price=1e18 for 6-decimal collateral, fuzz collateral amount and debt, assert liquidate always reverts with NotLiquidatable.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / IOracle.price] Can an unprivileged market creator deploy a market whose CollateralParams.oracle returns a price 1e12x too low for a 27-decimal collateral token (oracle returns price per 1e27 unit instead of per 1 unit), causing isHealthy to compute maxDebt far below actual collateral value, making every borrower with that collateral immediately liquidatable even when fully overcollateralized? Preconditions: market with 27-decimal collateral, oracle price = 1e9 (correct for 1:1 with 18-decimal loan token per 1e27 collateral unit) but collateral stored in raw units; borrower supplies 1e27 units (1 token). Call sequence: touchMarket -> supplyCollateral(1e27) -> take (borrow at LLTV) -> isHealthy returns false -> liquidate succeeds. Invariant tested: healthy accounts must not become liquidatable due to oracle/decimal edge cases. Scoped impact: valid borrowers become liquidatable, collateral seized without valid cause. Proof idea: unit test with 27-decimal MockERC20 and oracle returning price=1e9, assert isHealthy(borrower) == false for a position that should be healthy.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: liquidate / IOracle.price] Can an unprivileged liquidator trigger a revert in liquidate when the oracle for the liquidated collateral (collateralIndex) returns 0, because seizedAssets > 0 path computes repaidUnits = seizedAssets.mulDivUp(liquidatedCollatPrice, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, lif) with liquidatedCollatPrice=0 yielding repaidUnits=0, and then repaidUnits > 0 path computes seizedAssets = repaidUnits.mulDivDown(lif, WAD).mulDivDown(ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE, liquidatedCollatPrice) causing division by zero? Preconditions: borrower is unhealthy (other collateral oracle returns normal price); liquidated collateral oracle returns 0; liquidator calls liquidate with seizedAssets > 0. Call sequence: touchMarket -> supplyCollateral(collateralIndex=0) -> take (borrow) -> oracle[0].price() returns 0 -> liquidate(seizedAssets=X, repaidUnits=0) reverts. Invariant tested: unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable; oracle returning 0 must not permanently freeze liquidation. Scoped impact: withdrawals/liquidations revert unexpectedly, unhealthy position cannot be liquidated. Proof idea: MockOracle.price() returns 0, assert liquidate with seizedAssets>0 reverts, assert liquidate with repaidUnits>0 also reverts via division by zero.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: liquidate / IOracle.price] Can an unprivileged borrower activate a collateral whose oracle always reverts (e.g., oracle contract self-destructs or always throws), then because supplyCollateral does not call the oracle, the borrower's collateralBitmap has a bit set for that collateral, causing every subsequent call to isHealthy, withdrawCollateral (when debt>0), take (when seller has debt), and liquidate to revert, permanently freezing the borrower's position? Preconditions: borrower supplies collateral at index i with a reverting oracle; borrower has nonzero debt; collateralBitmap bit i is set. Call sequence: supplyCollateral(i, assets) -> take (borrow) -> oracle[i] starts reverting -> isHealthy reverts -> liquidate reverts -> withdrawCollateral reverts. Invariant tested: unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable; fund freeze must not be achievable by oracle revert. Scoped impact: liquidations revert unexpectedly, valid borrower's collateral frozen. Proof idea: deploy RevertingOracle, assert all health-checking functions revert after oracle starts reverting.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / liquidate] Can a multi-collateral borrower with 16 activated collaterals (MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER) cause liquidate to revert if any one of the 16 oracles reverts, even if the borrower is deeply unhealthy and the liquidator only wants to seize collateral from a non-reverting collateral index? Preconditions: borrower has 16 collaterals activated; oracle at index k reverts; borrower is unhealthy. Call sequence: supplyCollateral x16 -> take (borrow) -> oracle[k].price() reverts -> liquidate(collateralIndex != k) reverts because the bitmap loop calls all 16 oracles. Invariant tested: unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable regardless of individual oracle failures. Scoped impact: liquidations revert unexpectedly, bad debt cannot be realized. Proof idea: fuzz which oracle index reverts, assert liquidate always reverts when any bitmap oracle reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: liquidate (bitmap loop) / IOracle.price] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate with a collateralIndex that is NOT set in the borrower's collateralBitmap, causing liquidatedCollatPrice to remain 0 after the bitmap loop, and then the seizedAssets > 0 branch to compute repaidUnits = seizedAssets.mulDivUp(0, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, lif) = 0, effectively allowing the liquidator to seize collateral for 0 repaid units? Preconditions: borrower is unhealthy; collateralIndex is a valid market index but not in borrower's bitmap; borrower has nonzero collateral[collateralIndex] from a prior deposit that cleared the bitmap bit. Call sequence: supplyCollateral(i) -> withdrawCollateral(i, all) -> take (borrow using other collateral) -> liquidate(collateralIndex=i, seizedAssets=X) with liquidatedCollatPrice=0. Invariant tested: repaidUnits must correctly reflect seized collateral value; collateral must not be seized for free. Scoped impact: direct loss, unauthorized collateral seizure. Proof idea: unit test asserting repaidUnits > 0 whenever seizedAssets > 0.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / mulDivDown rounding] Can systematic rounding-down in maxDebt += collateral[i].mulDivDown(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivDown(lltv, WAD) across 16 collaterals cause a borrower whose true maxDebt equals debt to appear unhealthy (maxDebt < debt by accumulated rounding), making a healthy position liquidatable? Preconditions: borrower has 16 collaterals each contributing exactly 1 unit of rounding loss; debt == true maxDebt. Call sequence: supplyCollateral x16 (amounts chosen to maximize rounding loss) -> take (borrow at exact LLTV) -> isHealthy returns false -> liquidate succeeds. Invariant tested: healthy accounts must not become liquidatable due to rounding. Scoped impact: valid borrowers become liquidatable. Proof idea: fuzz collateral amounts and prices to maximize cumulative rounding loss, assert isHealthy(borrower) == true when debt == floor(true maxDebt).

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / withdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged borrower with a near-threshold position trigger a revert in withdrawCollateral when the oracle returns a price such that collateral * price overflows uint256 before division by ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE in mulDivDown(collateral[i], price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE), causing withdrawCollateral to revert unexpectedly even though the borrower is healthy? Preconditions: collateral amount near type(uint128).max; oracle returns price near type(uint256).max / type(uint128).max; borrower has nonzero debt. Call sequence: supplyCollateral(type(uint128).max) -> take (borrow) -> oracle.price() returns large value -> withdrawCollateral reverts due to overflow in mulDivDown. Invariant tested: valid withdrawals must not revert due to oracle price overflow. Scoped impact: withdrawals revert unexpectedly, fund freeze. Proof idea: fuzz collateral and price values near overflow boundary, assert mulDivDown(collateral, price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE) does not overflow for any valid uint128 collateral and uint256 price.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: liquidate / maxDebt accumulation overflow] Can an unprivileged borrower with multiple high-value collaterals cause the maxDebt accumulation loop in liquidate to overflow uint256 when summing collateral[i].mulDivDown(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivDown(lltv, WAD) across all activated collaterals, causing liquidate to revert unexpectedly even though the borrower is unhealthy? Preconditions: borrower has multiple collaterals each with collateral amount near type(uint128).max and oracle price near ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE; sum of maxDebt contributions overflows uint256. Call sequence: supplyCollateral x N -> take (borrow) -> liquidate reverts due to overflow in maxDebt += ... Invariant tested: unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable; overflow must not freeze liquidation. Scoped impact: liquidations revert unexpectedly. Proof idea: fuzz N collaterals with large amounts and prices, assert maxDebt accumulation never overflows for valid uint128 collateral and uint256 price.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / take (seller health check)] Can an unprivileged taker cause take to revert at the final isHealthy check (line 476) when the seller's oracle returns a price that makes collateral * price > type(uint128).max (as documented in LIVENESS), even though the seller is healthy, because the intermediate mulDivDown result exceeds uint128 before being compared to debt? Preconditions: seller has debt and collateral; oracle returns price such that collateral * price / ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE > type(uint128).max; seller is actually healthy. Call sequence: supplyCollateral -> take (borrow) -> oracle price increases -> taker calls take on seller's offer -> isHealthy reverts -> take reverts. Invariant tested: valid take operations must not revert due to oracle price range. Scoped impact: withdrawals/liquidations (and takes) revert unexpectedly. Proof idea: unit test with oracle returning price = type(uint256).max / collateral + 1, assert isHealthy reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: liquidate / badDebt calculation asymmetry] Can the asymmetry between badDebt using mulDivUp(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, maxLif) and maxDebt using mulDivDown(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivDown(lltv, WAD) cause a position where badDebt > 0 but the position is actually healthy (maxDebt >= debt), allowing a liquidator to realize bad debt and slash lenders on a healthy borrower? Preconditions: borrower is at exact health boundary; oracle price chosen such that mulDivUp overestimates collateral value in badDebt but mulDivDown underestimates in maxDebt; debt == maxDebt (rounded down). Call sequence: supplyCollateral -> take (borrow at exact LLTV) -> liquidate(seizedAssets=0, repaidUnits=0) to realize bad debt -> lender credit slashed. Invariant tested: healthy accounts must not have bad debt realized against them. Scoped impact: unauthorized seizure of lender credit, accounting corruption. Proof idea: fuzz price and collateral at health boundary, assert badDebt == 0 whenever maxDebt >= debt.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: liquidate / RCF maxRepaid with oracle price] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the maxRepaid calculation maxRepaid = (debt - maxDebt).mulDivUp(WAD * WAD, WAD * WAD - lif * lltv) where maxDebt uses the oracle price, such that if the oracle returns a slightly inflated price, maxDebt is inflated, (debt - maxDebt) is smaller, maxRepaid is smaller, and the RCF blocks the liquidator from repaying enough to restore health, leaving the position permanently stuck between unhealthy and the RCF cap? Preconditions: borrower is unhealthy; oracle returns price slightly above true price; lltv and lif chosen such that WAD * WAD - lif * lltv is small; maxRepaid < amount needed to restore health. Call sequence: supplyCollateral -> take (borrow) -> oracle inflates price slightly -> liquidate(repaidUnits=X) reverts with RecoveryCloseFactorConditionsViolated for any X that would restore health. Invariant tested: unhealthy positions must be liquidatable to health. Scoped impact: liquidation failure, fund freeze. Proof idea: fuzz oracle price near true value, assert there always exists a valid repaidUnits that passes RCF and restores health.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / fee-on-transfer collateral token] Can an unprivileged borrower use a fee-on-transfer collateral token where safeTransferFrom pulls X assets but only X - fee arrive at Midnight, while collateral[collateralIndex] records X, causing isHealthy to compute maxDebt based on X (overstated collateral), making the borrower appear healthier than reality and allowing undercollateralized borrowing? Preconditions: collateral token charges 1% fee on transfer; borrower calls supplyCollateral(assets=100); collateral[i] = 100 but actual balance increase = 99; borrower borrows at LLTV based on 100 units of collateral. Call sequence: supplyCollateral(100) -> collateral[i]=100, actual balance+=99 -> take (borrow at LLTV of 100) -> isHealthy uses 100 -> position is actually undercollateralized. Invariant tested: collateral accounting must match actual token balances; IERC20 interface has no mechanism to detect fee-on-transfer. Scoped impact: invalid borrowers stay healthy, bad debt. Proof idea: deploy FeeOnTransferERC20 as collateral, assert collateral[i] > actual balance after supplyCollateral.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / rebasing collateral token] Can an unprivileged borrower use a rebasing collateral token where the token balance held by Midnight increases without any supplyCollateral call, but collateral[collateralIndex] does not update, causing isHealthy to undercount the borrower's actual collateral value and making a healthy borrower appear liquidatable? Preconditions: collateral token rebases upward (e.g., stETH-like); Midnight holds more tokens than collateral[i] records; borrower is at LLTV boundary. Call sequence: supplyCollateral(X) -> token rebases, Midnight balance = X + delta -> isHealthy uses collateral[i]=X not X+delta -> maxDebt < debt -> borrower appears unhealthy -> liquidate succeeds. Invariant tested: healthy accounts must not become liquidatable due to token balance changes not reflected in collateral accounting. Scoped impact: valid borrowers become liquidatable, unauthorized collateral seizure. Proof idea: deploy RebasingERC20, trigger rebase, assert isHealthy still returns true for a position that should be healthy.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / rebasing collateral token (downward)] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit a downward-rebasing collateral token where Midnight's actual balance decreases below collateral[i], causing isHealthy to overcount collateral value (using stale collateral[i] > actual balance), making an undercollateralized borrower appear healthy and preventing liquidation? Preconditions: collateral token rebases downward; collateral[i] = X but actual Midnight balance = X - delta; borrower's true maxDebt < debt. Call sequence: supplyCollateral(X) -> token rebases down -> isHealthy uses collateral[i]=X -> maxDebt overstated -> borrower appears healthy -> liquidate reverts with NotLiquidatable. Invariant tested: unhealthy positions must be liquidatable; IERC20 interface has no mechanism to detect rebasing. Scoped impact: invalid borrowers stay healthy, bad debt accumulates. Proof idea: deploy DownwardRebasingERC20, trigger rebase, assert liquidate succeeds when true collateral value < debt.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / LLTV=1 (WAD) special case] Can an unprivileged borrower in a market with LLTV=1e18 (WAD) exploit the fact that maxDebt = collateral * price / ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE * WAD / WAD = collateral * price / ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE, and if the oracle returns price = ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE (1e36), then maxDebt = collateral, so any borrower with debt <= collateral is healthy regardless of actual market value, allowing borrowing of loan tokens worth more than the collateral's true value if the oracle is misconfigured? Preconditions: LLTV=1e18; oracle returns price=1e36 (1:1 ratio); collateral token is worth less than loan token in reality. Call sequence: touchMarket(lltv=1e18) -> supplyCollateral(100) -> take (borrow 100 units) -> isHealthy returns true (maxDebt=100=debt) -> borrower has loan tokens worth more than collateral. Invariant tested: debt must not exceed true collateral value; LLTV=1 has no overcollateralization buffer. Scoped impact: bad debt, invalid borrowers stay healthy. Proof idea: unit test with LLTV=1, oracle price=1e36, assert health check is correct for various collateral/debt ratios.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: liquidate / post-maturity lif ramp with oracle price] Can an unprivileged liquidator in post-maturity mode exploit the lif ramp lif = min(maxLif, WAD + (maxLif - WAD) * (block.timestamp - maturity) / TIME_TO_MAX_LIF) combined with oracle price to compute seizedAssets = repaidUnits.mulDivDown(lif, WAD).mulDivDown(ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE, liquidatedCollatPrice) where a very small liquidatedCollatPrice causes seizedAssets to overflow uint256, reverting the liquidation? Preconditions: post-maturity; oracle returns very small price (e.g., 1); repaidUnits near type(uint128).max; lif near maxLif. Call sequence: take (borrow) -> warp past maturity -> oracle.price() returns 1 -> liquidate(postMaturityMode=true, repaidUnits=large) -> seizedAssets = large * maxLif / WAD * 1e36 / 1 overflows. Invariant tested: post-maturity liquidations must not revert due to arithmetic overflow. Scoped impact: liquidations revert unexpectedly. Proof idea: fuzz repaidUnits and oracle price, assert seizedAssets computation never overflows.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / multi-collateral decimal mismatch] Can an unprivileged market creator create a multi-collateral market where collateral[0] is 18-decimal and collateral[1] is 6-decimal, and the oracle for collateral[1] returns price in 18-decimal units (not adjusted for 6-decimal token), causing isHealthy to compute maxDebt contribution for collateral[1] as 1e12x too high, making borrowers with only collateral[1] appear 1e12x healthier than reality while borrowers with only collateral[0] are correctly assessed? Preconditions: multi-collateral market; oracle[1] returns price not adjusted for 6-decimal token; borrower uses only collateral[1]. Call sequence: touchMarket(collateralParams=[18dec, 6dec]) -> supplyCollateral(index=1, assets=1e6) -> take (borrow 1e18 units) -> isHealthy returns true (maxDebt = 1e6 * 1e18 / 1e36 * lltv = 1e6 * lltv >> debt). Invariant tested: multi-collateral valuation must correctly account for token decimals. Scoped impact: invalid borrowers stay healthy, bad debt. Proof idea: differential test comparing isHealthy result with and without decimal adjustment in oracle.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / msb bitmap iteration order] Can the msb-based bitmap iteration in isHealthy (processing collaterals from highest index to lowest) interact with oracle price ordering such that if the highest-index oracle reverts, all lower-index collateral contributions are skipped, making a borrower with valid lower-index collateral appear to have maxDebt=0 and become liquidatable? Preconditions: borrower has collaterals at indices 0 and 15; oracle[15] reverts; oracle[0] returns valid price; borrower is healthy based on collateral[0] alone. Call sequence: supplyCollateral(0) -> supplyCollateral(15) -> take (borrow) -> oracle[15].price() reverts -> isHealthy reverts (not returns false) -> withdrawCollateral reverts. Invariant tested: oracle revert for one collateral must not prevent health assessment of other collaterals. Scoped impact: withdrawals revert unexpectedly, valid borrower's position frozen. Proof idea: unit test with 2 collaterals, assert isHealthy behavior when highest-index oracle reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: liquidate / liquidatedCollatPrice vs isHealthy price divergence] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that oracle.price() is called twice in liquidate (once in the bitmap loop for health check, once implicitly via liquidatedCollatPrice assignment), and if the oracle returns different values on consecutive calls within the same transaction (e.g., a stateful oracle), the health check uses price P1 but seizedAssets/repaidUnits are computed using price P2, causing the liquidator to seize more collateral than the repaid debt justifies? Preconditions: oracle is stateful and returns different values on consecutive calls; first call returns low price (position appears unhealthy), second call returns high price (liquidatedCollatPrice is high, so seizedAssets is large for given repaidUnits). Call sequence: deploy StatefulOracle -> take (borrow) -> oracle state changes -> liquidate -> first price call makes position unhealthy, second price call inflates seizedAssets. Invariant tested: seized collateral value must equal repaid debt value times LIF; oracle price must be consistent within a liquidation. Scoped impact: direct loss, unauthorized collateral seizure. Proof idea: deploy StatefulOracle that alternates prices, assert seizedAssets * price / ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE <= repaidUnits * lif.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / ERC777 hook on collateral] Can an unprivileged borrower use an ERC777-compatible collateral token that triggers a tokensReceived hook on safeTransferFrom, allowing the borrower to reenter Midnight during supplyCollateral and call take to borrow before collateral[i] is updated, then have the hook complete the collateral deposit, resulting in a borrow that was health-checked before collateral was recorded? Preconditions: collateral token is ERC777; borrower's tokensReceived hook calls take to borrow; supplyCollateral calls safeTransferFrom which triggers hook before collateral[i] is updated. Call sequence: supplyCollateral -> safeTransferFrom triggers hook -> hook calls take (borrow) -> isHealthy called with collateral[i]=0 -> take reverts with SellerIsLiquidatable OR succeeds if debt=0. Invariant tested: collateral must be recorded before health check in borrow path; IERC20 interface does not prevent ERC777 hooks. Scoped impact: bad debt, unauthorized borrowing. Proof idea: deploy ERC777Collateral, assert take during supplyCollateral hook cannot create undercollateralized debt.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: withdrawCollateral / isHealthy oracle revert] Can an unprivileged borrower permanently freeze their own collateral withdrawal by activating a second collateral whose oracle later starts reverting, since withdrawCollateral calls isHealthy which iterates all bitmap collaterals and reverts if any oracle reverts, even if the borrower wants to withdraw the non-reverting collateral? Preconditions: borrower has collateral[0] (oracle OK) and collateral[1] (oracle reverts); borrower has debt; borrower wants to withdraw collateral[0]. Call sequence: supplyCollateral(0) -> supplyCollateral(1) -> take (borrow) -> oracle[1] starts reverting -> withdrawCollateral(0) -> isHealthy reverts -> withdrawal reverts. Invariant tested: valid withdrawals must not be permanently blocked by unrelated oracle failures. Scoped impact: withdrawals revert unexpectedly, fund freeze. Proof idea: unit test asserting withdrawCollateral(0) reverts when oracle[1] reverts, even if collateral[0] alone covers debt.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / price = 1 (minimum non-zero)] Can an unprivileged market creator configure an oracle that returns price=1 (minimum non-zero uint256), causing maxDebt = collateral * 1 / 1e36 * lltv / 1e18 = 0 for any collateral amount below 1e36, making every borrower with that collateral immediately liquidatable regardless of how much collateral they supply? Preconditions: oracle returns price=1; collateral amount < 1e36 (always true since collateral is uint128 max ~3.4e38, but for typical amounts < 1e36); borrower has any debt. Call sequence: touchMarket(oracle returning 1) -> supplyCollateral(1e18) -> take (borrow 1 unit) -> isHealthy: maxDebt = 1e18 * 1 / 1e36 * lltv / 1e18 = 0 < 1 -> liquidatable. Invariant tested: healthy accounts must not become liquidatable due to oracle price edge cases. Scoped impact: valid borrowers become liquidatable. Proof idea: unit test with oracle.price()=1, assert isHealthy returns false for any reasonable collateral amount.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / price = type(uint256).max] Can an unprivileged market creator configure an oracle that returns price=type(uint256).max, causing mulDivDown(collateral, type(uint256).max, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE) to overflow uint256 in isHealthy, reverting all health checks and permanently blocking liquidations, withdrawals, and takes for any borrower in that market? Preconditions: oracle returns type(uint256).max; borrower has any collateral and debt. Call sequence: touchMarket -> supplyCollateral -> take (borrow) -> oracle.price() returns type(uint256).max -> isHealthy reverts due to overflow -> liquidate reverts -> withdrawCollateral reverts. Invariant tested: oracle price overflow must not permanently freeze protocol operations. Scoped impact: liquidations and withdrawals revert unexpectedly. Proof idea: unit test with oracle returning type(uint256).max, assert isHealthy reverts, assert no valid liquidation path exists.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Oracle edge-case bug causing valid borrowers to become liquidatable, invalid borrowers to stay healthy, or withdrawals/liquidations to revert unexpectedly'] [Function: isHealthy / token with 0 decimals] Can an unprivileged market creator create a market with a 0-decimal collateral token (1 unit = 1 whole token) where the oracle returns price=1e36 (1:1 with loan token), causing isHealthy to compute maxDebt = 1 * 1e36 / 1e36 * lltv = lltv for 1 unit of collateral, which is correct, but if the oracle is configured for 18-decimal assumptions and returns price=1e18, maxDebt = 1 * 1e18 / 1e36 * lltv = 0 for 1 unit, making every borrower liquidatable? Preconditions: 0-decimal collateral; oracle returns price=1e18 (wrong scale); borrower supplies 1 unit and borrows. Call sequence: touchMarket -> supplyCollateral(1) -> take (borrow 1 unit) -> isHealthy: maxDebt = 1 * 1e18 / 1e36 * lltv = 0 -> liquidatable. Invariant tested: IERC20 interface has no decimals(), oracle must encode decimal normalization; misconfigured oracle causes healthy borrower to be liquidatable. Scoped impact: valid borrowers become liquidatable. Proof idea: unit test with 0-decimal token and oracle returning 1e18, assert isHealthy returns false.
